data.title: July Jobs Report Stuns Wall Street, Fed Rate Cut In Play, Key Charts
data.description:
The July jobs report came in much weaker than expected. Just 73,000 positions were added, below the +110,000 consensus, but the real stunner was the sharp negative revisions to May and June payrolls. The previous two months were taken down by 258,000, the largest shaving since May 2020.

The unemployment rate climbed to the highest level since October 2021, at 4.248%. So, it was a whisker from rounding up to 4.3%. Average hourly earnings rose by 0.3%, right on with the Wall Street target, while the year-on-year worker pay growth rate is now 3.9%, which was ahead of estimates. A bright spot was a 0.1-hour jump in the average weekly hours worked, helping to offset the light headline payrolls addition.

Private payrolls were also soft at just 83,000; June’s figure was revised lower to just 3,000. Government and manufacturing employment contracted in July, while the U-6 underemployment rate rose from 7.7% to 7.9%.

There was a steep decline in the number of foreign-born workers--that's a key story since lower immigration numbers will bring down the break-even pace of job growth needed to keep the unemployment rate steady. It may now be in the 70k-90k range.

After the dust settled, the odds of a September rate cut jumped from 40% to 69%, and the chorus of easing from Fed officials could grow louder and expand from just Governors Waller and Bowman.

In terms of market price action, the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) plummeted to under 3.8% in the minutes after the 8:30 a.m. ET data, down more than 15 basis points. Stocks actually barely budged, but the US Dollar Index (DXY) plunged 1.6%, with the EURUSD (EUR:USD) currency pair climbing from $1.14 to $1.156. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) spiked 1.8%, and bitcoin (BTC-USD) was steady. The S&P VIX Index (VIX) neared 19.

A Weak July Jobs Report

Unemployment Rate Climbs to the Highest Rate Since October 2021, 3-Month Average Payroll Growth Slowest Since June 2020

Jobless Rate Rises 0.13%

Private Payrolls Also Soft

All the Employment Gain Came from Education & Health Services

Two-Month Revisions: Worst Since 2020

Household Survey Negative

69% Chance of A Sept Cut, Two Quarter-Point Eases Priced Into 2025

Recession Risk Rises from 10% to 13%

X Poll: Fintwit Was Close

Stocks Little Changed After the NFP, -1%

2-Year Yield Plunges

Dollar Drops Hard After A Steep Rally

President Trump May Be Right

Looking ahead, a light data week is on tap after the onslaught of macro volatility catalysts in recent days. Factory Orders and the Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending (SLOOS) hit Monday.

June trade data, a final look at S&P Global PMIs, and July ISM Services come Tuesday. Claims and productivity figures, along with the NY Fed’s Consumer Inflation Survey, arrive on Thursday—that's also when President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs are set to take effect.

Key Data On Tap

The Bottom Line

After mainly solid jobs reports in 2025, the July employment situation was much weaker than expected. The headline payrolls climb fell far short of economists’ estimates, and revisions to May and June were dreadful. This report pressures Chair Powell and the Fed to consider resuming rate cuts in September.

data.title: Clarivate: Upside Is Attractive If Execution Holds
data.description:
Investment action

I gave a buy rating for Clarivate (NYSE:CLVT) previously, as I thought the business was going to see organic growth inflect upwards, and should momentum keep up, the stock should re-rate higher. With 2Q25 marking the second straight quarter of organic growth and further acceleration in ACV and recurring revenue, I believe the thesis is playing out. The underlying momentum across all three segments, combined with clear adoption traction for CLVT’s AI tools, reinforces my view that this turnaround is real. At ~5.7x NTM PE, the stock still looks cheap, and I continue to see material upside if execution stays on track.

2Q25 Earnings Review

CLVT's 2Q25 performance added further credibility to the turnaround narrative. While revenue of $621.4 million did fall by 4.4% y/y, this shouldn’t be alarming since it was entirely driven by planned portfolio rationalization. The more important figure is organic revenue, which grew 0.5% y/y, marking the second straight quarter of positive growth and an acceleration from the 0.3% seen in 1Q25. This improvement was also fairly broad-based. Academia & Government [A&G] stood out with 2.5% organic growth, while Life Sciences & Healthcare [LS&H] returned to growth at 0.3%. Even though the IP segment declined 2.1% organically, there were signs of recovery in its core patent renewals business. Profitability was also impressive. Adj. EBITDA hit $261.6 million, translating to a strong 42.1% margin, and adj. EPS came in at $0.18.

Organic Growth Inflection Is Forming A Credible Trend

After looking at the 2Q25 performance, I believe we can now say this organic growth inflection is becoming a credible trend. More importantly, forward-looking indicators are looking very healthy. Organic Annualized Contract Value [ACV], which I take as the best measure of momentum in the core subscription business, accelerated to 1.3% growth. This is a 40 bps improvement from FY24 and also shows that adoption is gradually picking up. The quality of this growth is also important to note, and the metric to monitor here is recurring organic revenue, which strips out the more volatile transactional business. This metric accelerated to 0.8% growth in the quarter (up from 0.6% y/y in 1Q25). Put together, I think it really shows that CLVT’s Value Creation Plan is successfully re-engineering the business model toward higher-quality, more predictable revenue. And if this becomes a sustainable trend over the coming quarters, CLVT should earn a higher valuation multiple from the market.

Underlying Business Health Is Stronger Than Ever

Now, if we dig further into other operating metrics, the turnaround story looks even more credible. One of the clearest signs is the improvement in the annual renewal rate, which ticked up to 93% in 1H25 from 92% vs. last year. While that may not seem like a lot, it is actually very meaningful in a subscription-based model because it reflects (1) the value proposition continues to resonate, (2) CLVT sales execution remains healthy, and (3) customer lifetime value [CLTV] goes up, which enhances CLVT’s long-term profit runway.

The other impressive thing is the transformation in CLVT’s revenue mix. As CLVT continues to pivot away from lower-quality transactional revenue, CLVT organic recurring revenue mix is now at 88%, up 800 bps vs. last year and a 500 bps step up from 1Q25. I believe this has further de-risked the business fundamentally, and it is logical because higher recurring revenue brings better visibility and more stable cash flow.

Finally, it was also very good to see that the breadth of the recovery was supported by all three core segments, validating that the turnaround is not just a one-off but the result of sound execution across the board. For reference, A&G saw 2% organic ACV and subscription revenue growth in 1H25, despite budget pressures in its end markets. Importantly, 75% of A&G’s FY25 subscription base had already been renewed by July, and this provides strong revenue visibility going into 2H25. In the IP segment, the rebound in patent renewals (which I believe is the most important recurring revenue stream) is notable. Organic growth of ~1.5% in 1H25 signals stabilization, and I think this is an early inning of further growth acceleration as CLVT is well positioned to benefit from the structural increase in patent filings being driven by the AI boom. On the other hand, LS&H investments are clearly starting to pay off. The return to organic ACV growth in 1H25, driven by improved retention in the Cortellis suite, suggests that customer demand has improved, and if that holds, subscription revenue should follow.

AI Strategy Is Delivering Tangible Results and a Competitive Edge

In my May write-up, I discussed that CLVT’s AI strategy is no longer just talk. I have even more conviction this is true after looking at the progress made in 2Q25. As of 2Q25, more than 4,800 institutions are actively using CLVT’s AI-powered tools, up from more than 3,000 in 1Q25, clearly suggesting that there is a strong product-market fit. The fact that research firm Outsell named Clarivate as the “industry leader” in developing agentic AI tools for scholarly workflows also shows that product-market fit is solid.

In my view, the most important aspect here is how AI is being embedded into core platforms like Web of Science, especially in features with high utilization like the literature review agent. As I said before, these tools solve real pain points by saving researchers time and improving accuracy, which ultimately boosts the overall value proposition of the CLVT platform. I also mentioned that this would drive higher customer stickiness and lower churn, but one additional area I am convinced CLVT can benefit from is that this also opens the door for upselling premium AI tiers. As more users understand the value and restructure their workflow to leverage AI, CLVT could roll out AI tiers (with better features, etc.) to meaningfully expand ARPU.

Valuation

When I said CLVT was cheap at 6x NTM PE, I didn’t expect it to get cheaper (now at 5.7x) despite the improvements in fundamentals. I believe the market has not fully digested the improvement, as the headline revenue decline did not screen well. Like I have said above, organic growth continues to inflect, underlying operating metrics are getting healthier, and CLVT’s AI strategy is shaping up to be a real growth driver. All of these should warrant multiples to go higher, and at this valuation, even a modest upgrade to the -1 standard deviation level of 7.8x NTM PE would yield amazing upside. I remind readers that Dun & Bradstreet Holdings (DNB) trades at a premium to CLVT with similar revenue growth rates, so that is a precedent that the market is willing to attach a higher multiple.

Risk

Despite the encouraging progress, CLVT turnaround is still in its early phase. Organic growth, while improving, remains low and heavily reliant on execution of its Value Creation Plan. External risks are also notable, particularly the threat of university and government funding cuts, which could pressure A&G demand. Lastly, the ongoing strategic review introduces uncertainty, with no resolution expected until early 2026. While the direction of travel is positive, these risks mean the investment case still carries execution and macro risk.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for CLVT. The business is clearly turning a corner, with organic growth now stabilizing and key forward indicators trending in the right direction. The revenue mix is shifting toward higher-quality recurring streams, and AI adoption is progressing well. With improving fundamentals and a cheap valuation, I see a compelling upside profile. Execution remains the key, but if CLVT delivers, I believe the stock is well-positioned for a meaningful re-rating.

data.title: S&P500: Euphoria And Bubble Face The New World Reality
data.description:
The context

The S&P500 (SP500) is entering August trading at a bubble-like valuation second only to the peak of the 2000 dot-com bubble, based on the Shiller PE ratio.

Consistent with the high valuation, the sentiment is exuberant based on many measures, which points to euphoria.

At the same time, the global economy is entering a new protectionist state with the increase in tariffs on August 1st, which is widely expected to cause an increase in inflation and slower growth over the next two quarters, with significant stagflationary implications.

Thus, the bubble in the S&P500 is likely to burst as the euphoria fizzles with the first obvious signs of stagflation, which is already creeping up in data, but currently ignored by the market participants.

Signs of Euphoria

There are many measures of market sentiments, and they all point to the increased signs of euphoria in the market.

The Citi's Levkovich Index for example, measures margin levels, option pricing, and short interest, and it's showing the return on euphoria. The Index's threshold for euphoria is 0.38, and it hit 0.65 last Friday. In fact, this is the third highest level of euphoria, behind the 2000 dot.com bubble and 2021 post-pandemic retail-led meme stock mania.

Barclay's Equity Euphoria Indicator EEI measures the speculative flows in derivatives, futures, and options. Similarly to the Levkovich Index, the EEI is also indicating euphoria in the market just like at the peak of the 2000 dot.com bubble, and the post-pandemic meme stock mania.

The bubble

The S&P500 is trading at the bubble-like valuations, above the post-pandemic meme stock mania, and just below the 2000 dot-come bubble valuation, based on the Shiller PE ratio, which is currently just below 39.

Thus, the measures of euphoria match the bubble valuations.

Note, the 2025 forward ratio for the S&P500 is near 25. The bubble is led by the tech mega-caps, associated with the AI theme.

The argument is that these tech companies are producing significant profits, which was not the case in 2000 with the dot-coms. Thus, the current situation is not comparable with 2000. However, the fact is that those earnings are overpriced by the market, by a large amount, and that's reflected in the PE ratio. The PE ratio simply normalizes the price with earnings, and these tech earnings are overpriced. The Information Technology ETF (XLK) is trading at a 2025 forward ratio of 33, so these expected 2025 earnings are very expensive.

But it is not just the tech that's overpriced. Costco (COST) is trading at a PE ratio of 53. WalMart's (WMT) PE is 38. Starbucks (SBUX) has a forward PE ratio of 41. Thus, the bubble is much broader. These are all large consumer-oriented companies that should not be trading at these valuations, given their growth prospects.

Note, Consumer Staples ETF (XLP) is trading at a PE ratio of 22, and this is supposed to be a defensive sector, a value play. Given the valuation, even the staples are expensive.

The new world reality

The Trump administration has been imposing tariffs on the US trading partners gradually since February.

First, in February, the Trump administration imposed introductory tariffs on China, in addition to Mexico and Canada. Then on April 2nd, the Trump administration imposed the 10% universal tariff, with some sector-based tariffs, and additional country-based tariffs. Finally, on August 1st, the US imposed the reciprocal tariffs, with an increase in the universal tariff to 15% for countries that have trade deficits with the US.

The average total US tariff now is somewhere between 15% and 20%, probably closer to 20% - and that's very high compared to virtually no tariffs last year.

In addition, tariffs are expected to keep increasing. There are still some sector-based tariffs that are expected to be imposed, like semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. The trade deal with China is still being negotiated, and it's likely that tariffs on China could increase. Specifically, the 50% secondary tariffs on countries trading with Russia are expected to be imposed "soon", which could affect China and India.

The fact is that the free trade regime has been replaced with protectionism - and this is expected to boost inflation and slow growth. The tariffs seem to be connected with the escalating geopolitical situation, based on the tariffs on Brazil, and the secondary tariffs. That's the new word reality.

Implications

The market participants have been ignoring the "new world reality" since the selloff in response to the April 2nd tariffs. There are three reasons for this:

First, the market participants believed that Trump would negotiate trade deals by the August 1st deadline and that the universal tariff would remain at 10% - there would be no increase in tariffs. This thesis has now been proven wrong - tariffs substantially increased on August 1st, and will like to keep climbing.

Market participants were encouraged by the fact that the labor market has remained solid, and inflation has not spiked in response to the tariffs. Thus, the expectation was that the economy would remain resilient, and the Fed would be able to cut interest rates two times in 2025. The Fed has stated that it will continue to be data-dependent, which rules out the automatic interest rate cuts in 2025. Inflation is showing an early reaction to tariffs, with rising good prices, while the labor market is softening. Growth is slowing significantly, after adjusting for volatile net exports. More importantly, growth is expected to slow in Q3 after the front-loading of consumption ahead of tariffs in Q2.

The third reason is the AI trade. The AI capex is still strong and increasing, however, once the economy slows even the tech mega-caps will not be immune, given their exposure to consumer spending.

Over the next two quarters, inflation is likely to keep rising, while growth is likely to keep slowing, and it is very likely we could have an inflationary recession. The first confirming sign of deteriorating data is likely to bust the bubble in the S&P500 as the euphoria fizzles - and likely gets replaced with panic.

data.title: Rocket Lab Stock Surges With Potential Golden Dome Win (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) stock has surged nearly 75% since my June report significantly outperforming the S&P 500’s 7% gain. With the stock now trading well above my $34.22 price target, it is a good time to reassess the valuation and revisit catalysts for the stock price. In this report, I introduce an additional valuation framework to support my continued Buy rating.

Rocket Lab Shows Rapid Response Capability

Rocket Lab stock started its strong market outperformance in mid-June. From financial perspective, there were no noteworthy items that would warrant the stock price to run up as much as it did. I reviewed recent news items and could also not detect any major event. There, however, was one announcement that I consider to be a big positive. The company did announce two launches in 2025 for a confidential commercial customer. While not necessarily huge news, the announcement marked Rocket Lab’s ability to quickly go from contract to launch with the first launch scheduled just four days after the announcement. The second launch is also expected before the end of the year.

The company further signaled demand for more than 20 launches in 2025. I believe that the quick turn also provides support to an improving launch cadence. Launch schedules remain fluid, so adding two launches does not necessarily mean that the company will definitely launch two additional missions this year, but it possibly could which would also positively feather into the financial performance of the company.

Golden Dome Opportunity Could Be Huge

Rocket Lab has increasingly focused on improving its launch capabilities and its space systems capabilities. The development of the Neutron rocket allows the company to launch payloads in a different payload class while its expanding space systems portfolio positions the company more and more as a vertically integrated launch provider and a one-stop-shop solution. To that, we can add that Rocket Lab is increasingly focusing on providing solutions in the interest of national security and is also receiving contracts to compete for launch services adding further credibility to the company’s capabilities and position in the market.

I recently marked Rocket Lab as one of the companies that could benefit from the Golden Dome missile defense systems. I believe that Lockheed Martin (LMT) could be the lead integrator on the program. Rocket Lab could play a major role in terms of providing launch services and space systems engineering. Especially with the quarrel between President Trump and Elon Musk, I believe that Rocket Lab is a bit better positioned than Musk’s SpaceX to support the space-based parts of the missile defense program. That is not because SpaceX is not a capable launch provider, but because President Trump rather does not deal with companies or CEOs with whom he is not aligned and given that Musk threatened to decommission the Dragon spacecraft as part of his fallout with President Trump, it may not seem to be in the best national security interest to award SpaceX an exclusive contract and perhaps under the current conditions it may not be wise to include SpaceX at all.

That leads to a somewhat more prominent position for Rocket Lab to provide launch services and justifies an upbeat sentiment. At the same time, we do note that a lot is not known about the Golden Dome project. It is an ambitious project, but how it will be structured is not known. If space-based interceptors will be used, there is a huge opportunity for the likes of Rocket Lab but that is not a certainty as the budget and timeline in my view remain challenging.

Layering on top of the Golden Dome opportunity are opportunities in Europe as Europe is seeking space and security autonomy. Recently, Rocket Lab was selected to launch a pair of satellites for the LEO-PNT future navigation constellation.

Assessing The Price Target For Rocket Lab

Rocket Lab stock received a bullish coverage from Citi with a price target hike from $33 per share to $50 per share based on a more mature business in 2029. While I do not necessarily disagree with that, I do believe that basing the stock price target in 2025 on the expected results in 2029 is ambitious and perhaps only a suitable target for those who are extremely convinced of the company’s success in the long run and the patience required to get to that point.

I am not saying that the $50 target, which already has been reached in July, is not realistic but I do believe we should look at what stock prices are fundamentally supported in the nearer future. To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Normally, I use a price-to-sales method to provide preliminary assessment on the price target for companies that are not profitable yet. Rocket Lab is not expected to be profitable on EBITDA basis until 2026. Running a peer group valuation provides a $1.86 price target in 2026 and $9.47 for 2027. If we allow the company’s stock price to be driven by an EV/EBITDA multiple that is twice that of the peer group, we get a $18.90 price target. The lack of profits in the near term and the ramp in profits thereafter is clearly the reason why an EV/EBITDA valuation implies significant downside. As I believe that an enterprise value method better captures the capital structure of a company than a simple price-to-earnings or price-to-sales method for maturing companies, I have also considered an EV-to-sales valuation.

An EV/Sales valuation shows that in the years ahead, there is no tangible upside to the stock price. However, we do see support for the $50 price target that Citi analysts provided. In fact, our price target calculation exceeds $55 per share. At the same time, we do note that this only provides 21% upside from current levels and while I remain bullish on the long-term prospects for Rocket Lab, meaning post-2030, I do not believe that the strong buy rating can be maintained with only 21% upside by 2029. In other words: As bright as the prospects are for Rocket Lab, the stock price has run up a bit too much for a strong buy rating to be maintained and I am downgrading the stock to buy. Below a stock price of $41.22 and definitely below $28.68, a strong buy rating could be reapplied.

High Stock Price Leaves Little Room For Neutron Rocket Development Delays

While Rocket Lab is enjoying a lot of positive momentum with a growing national security market including Golden Dome opportunities, I do believe that risks such as the development of the Neutron rocket and the certification and associated timeline should also be considered and the current stock price provides extremely little padding for that. I am not marking the stock a sell, since there is no indication that Neutron rocket development is running behind on schedule but if it did there is a major risk in the current share price due to the recent surge.

Conclusion: Rocket Lab Has Upside For The Future

I definitely do like Rocket Lab as a company. That was the case years ago when I covered Rocket Lab even before it was public company and it is the case now. However, I also do recognize that the current stock price values the company years ahead of sales and earnings which invites risk. Investors have to be aware of these risks and weight the current valuation against the risks and timeline. I am downgrading the stock from strong buy to buy, noting both the long-term opportunities and the near-term schedule risks that exist with any launch vehicle development.

data.title: Securing $1,000 Monthly Cash Flow: Investments I'd Choose Now
data.description:
A frequent counterargument to income investing is that it takes a lot of capital before the key benefits of the strategy kick in.

For example, one of the main advantages of income investing is having access to recurring cash flows that could be opportunistically reinvested or matched with the relevant consumption needs.

If, say, we have a $50,000 portfolio that is placed in ~5% yielding assets, then, on a monthly basis, this would translate to ~$210, which is OK, but not great.

There are two broad ways how to solve this:

  1. Contributing more savings to the portfolio.
  2. Taking higher risk to get richer yields.

In my view, taking just one of these two routes is suboptimal.

My philosophy is to start with the second one - squeezing the most juice out of it - and once that is covered, I transition to the actual savings and allocations.

Starting with the yield-maximization is very critical to accelerate the process of snowballing income and hitting a point from which passive cash flows can be weaponized for material reinvestments (and thus incremental income growth) or consumption coverage. It is also important for manages the emotional biases - i.e., seeing fruits from the devoted efforts, which could motivate sticking on the same path or even doing more.

In this article I would like to share my approach for forming a $1,000 monthly cash flow generating portfolio. Why $1,000 and why monthly?

The answer is simple. In my opinion, achieving $1,000 per month of passive income is one of the first significant milestones for any income-seeking investor. So, I will show now how to get there in a fast and prudent fashion.

Sweet spot yield corridor

Here the initial answer could be: the higher the better.

In other words, the higher yields we get, the faster we can hit the $1,000 per month mark (or the lower capital requirement there is).

The problem with this "initial answer" is that it neglects the risk side.

We can take two quite popular high-yield names as examples: Prospect Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:PSEC) and Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC). Both of them provide 15%+ yields, pay them on a monthly basis, but over the past year or so have simply destroyed shareholder value and made unpleasant distribution cuts.

I think that it would be fair to say that we do not want to have these kinds of risks in our portfolio. Instead, the focus should be set towards:

  • Income durability so that we can count on what we buy and lock in initially.
  • Long-term capital protection so that we do not end up with eroded portfolio value (e.g., dividends being sponsored by return on of capital or NAV sales).

The typical sweet spot yield corridor to strike a balance between the two points above and yield maximization is 6% to 8%.

Just think of Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD), Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET), Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O), Main Street Capital Corporation (NYSE:MAIN) and other similar investment grade gems that are high-quality value businesses with very high probabilities of producing sustainable dividends going forward (and with no permanent capital impairments).

These all offer yields in and/or around the 6% to 8% range, which is still totally fine and make them solid candidates for any income-oriented investors' core holding base.

Yet, I see monthly paying investment opportunities, which produce income streams that are yielding closer and/or above 10%. All with acceptable risk profiles.

Getting first monthly $1,000 portfolio income streams is crucial, which could come at the expense of not necessarily capturing alpha or strong total returns as long as the cash flows are building up fast and are inherently sustainable.

Monthly paying aspect

The financial theory says that the dividend factor and especially the frequency of it do not drive value creation. It is all about total returns and whether part of these returns are distributed in income or accumulated as price gains is largely irrelevant.

I have to agree with this.

Yet, there is a difference between 100% rationale theory and real life. In practice, we as investors, are constantly subject to various emotional (and rationale) biases, which must be considered in the overall investment process.

For example, there could be investors out there for whom it is critical to have recurring cash flows to remain motivated and have simply more predictable financial plan in place. This is my case and I think I would not be wrong by stating that this applies for quite many investors as well.

In this context, the frequency factor is important. I find monthly income streams highly valuable in terms of allowing me to enhance my portfolio contribution amounts that I make each month by setting aside part of my salary. It also gives me the optionality to use these cash flows for consumption if suddenly such a need arises (without being forced to wait for the next quarter or, worst, liquidate a position in the portfolio).

5 picks

In the table below, you can see how by allocating less than $100,000, it is possible to access a bit more than $1,000 in monthly cash flows, while avoiding the assumption of aggressive risks.

Ticker Yield Amount Monthly cash Article
PFFA 9.5% $15,000 $119 Here
ASGI 11.9% $25,000 $248 Here
FSCO 11.1% $30,000 $278 Here
FEPI 26.5% $11,000 $243 Here
PTY 10.3% $15,000 $129 Here
Total $96,000 $1,016

I will now quickly and in a concise manner elaborate on why I have selected these picks (but you can see more details by clicking on my ticker-specific article references).

I have selected the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (NYSEARCA:PFFA) simply because it is a durable income machine with the right fundamentals to keep the cash distribution stable. PFFA has made consistent monthly payments since the IPO, with an exception of a small cut in the COVID-19. The cash flows could be deemed stable due to PFFA's exposure to preferred shares, which in combination with a healthy load of external leverage pushes the overall yield close to double digit territory. The beauty of preferred shares is that they stand above common equities in corporate capital structures. The companies that have issued preferred shares have to first stop dividend payments before they make adjustments to the preferred share cash flows. Given PFFA's diversification and active management, the risk of experiencing steep declines due to underperforming preferred share investments is low.

Abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI) is an infrastructure closed-end fund. The way how ASGI accommodates this high yield is through a targeted exposure to high-yielding infrastructure assets and the presence of external leverage. However, the key focus is on the former component as the leverage currently is immaterial (below 5%). Here, ASGI is also diversified: 30% industrials, 26% utilities, 8% oil and gas, and with the remaining exposure spread among telco towers, data centers and some other non-traditional infra plays. It is worth mentioning that all of the Top 10 holdings have either robust balance sheets or are backed by Government or notable private equity houses, which is an indicative of strong financial profiles. So, the combination of non-cyclical asset base and strong balance sheets introduces a favorable setting for sustainable (and high) distributions that are partly funded by realizing price gains.

FS Credit Opportunities Fund (NYSE:FSCO) is a closed-end fund with ~$2.1 billion in total assets, from which 73% are placed in private credit investments and the remaining is deployed in the public credit space. FSCO has avoided any income cuts since the IPO in November 2022, and, instead, it has made 5 hikes. If we look at the most recent earnings package, we will see that the distribution coverage ratio stands at 122% - i.e., a very strong margin of safety for ~11% yielding asset. Plus, FSCO has a conservative balance sheet with the debt to equity of ~0.6x - way below the BDC sector average of 1.17x. It is this margin of safety and diversification across mostly senior secured first lien loans with so prudent leverage that makes the income play sustainable here.

The REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:FEPI) is a covered call ETF, which is has invested in selected 15 picks from the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) area. These 15 stocks are comprised of the highest growth and mag-7 constitutes, which is exactly what is required to harvest rich call option premiums. By carrying a concentrated portfolio in the high growth and high volatility names, FEPI can sell options at more tangible premiums. This is why we see ~26% yield, which has largely remained stable since FEPI's inception. From all of my 5 picks, FEPI has obviously the highest risk. The risk here is linked to a potential Mag-7 drawdown, which would almost directly translate to NAV reduction for FEPI - albeit, there is some offset in place from the collected option premiums. In such a scenario, we would see dividend cuts as well. Yet, provided that the market trades sideways or even slightly/gradually goes down, the investment would be still worth it given the 25%+ dividends.

PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (NYSE:PTY) is a closed end fund that is managed by PIMCO and has, in my opinion, the most protected yield among other PIMCO CEF instruments. At its core, PTY is a traditional pure-play fixed income vehicle, where the assets are actively managed and partially sponsored by the external leverage. PTY has a well-diversified fixed income pool with the allocations nicely spread across different duration horizons, asset classes and more than 500 individual holdings. It has also one of the best trailing 6-month distribution coverage levels out there (based on PIMCO UNII report data). Finally, a very critical point to appreciate is the total effective leverage is 14.7%, which is significantly below the usual CEF average of 30% to 40%. This kind of leverage level definitely helps secure stability on the NII front (e.g., an optionality to increase the leverage in case the yields drop and dividend coverage shrinks).

Final remarks

The 5 picks that I presented above offer, in my view, one of the best yield-to-risk ratios out there. In other words, these 5 do the necessary job for getting the first $1,000 monthly cash flows fast, without excessive risks.

However, even though I have tried to maximize yield, we can see that there is still around $100,000 that is required.

So, with this in mind let me conclude the piece with the following quote from Berkshire Hathaway's legendary vice chairman Charlie Munger:

If it means walking everywhere and not eating anything that wasn't purchased with a coupon, find a way to get your hands on $100,000.

data.title: Coinbase: Q2 Disappointment Highlights Crypto Retail-To-Institution Transition
data.description:
Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN) announced Q2 earnings Thursday evening, disappointing investors with an adjusted EPS of $0.12, significantly below the $1.49 analyst consensus. Its net revenue came in at $1.42B, about 11% lower than expected. Losses were seen in most segments, with lower subscription and services sales, transaction revenue, and trading volumes. Its operating expenses also rose sharply at $1.52B, up ~37% from Q2 2024.

The report sent shares around 12% lower overnight, implying a significant miss. CEO Brian Armstrong focused on the continued expansion of new crypto assets and the integration of decentralized exchanges onto its app, with 24/7 trading of derivatives contracts on BTC and ETH. Its Q3 guidance remained strong, expecting expansion of its subscription and services segment, benefiting from the rebound in crypto prices, particularly the recent surge in Ethereum (ETH-USD).

Coinbase is one of my best and worst-performing analyses. I saw it as a bullish discount opportunity in early 2023 when it was worth around a tenth of its current price. However, I last covered it with a bearish outlook in the May 2024 article, "Coinbase: Cryptocurrencies Moving From 'New Paradigm' To Denial." Since then, it has risen by a staggering 68%, violating my bearish thesis.

So what went wrong? Most obviously, Bitcoin rose from around $60K to roughly $112K today, rising roughly as much as COIN. Although my bearish outlook has been, thus far, proven wrong, I stated:

Because enthusiasm remains strong, I would not be surprised to see Bitcoin hit $100K or more in 2024. I will not make that bet, but I would avoid betting against it or COIN as the apparent "cryptocurrency collective consciousness" seems quite eager to see a breakout.

I understood the futility of betting against the herd of cryptocurrency bulls. Enthusiasm became parabolic after Trump's election, viewing him as creating pro-crypto policies, pushing Bitcoin to $100K by year's end as anticipated. My view on Coinbase was long-term and not actionable on the short side for that reason. That said, I incorrectly thought that Bitcoin momentum was slowly; it did continue to fall briefly after May, but surged, most notably after the election.

My bearish thesis rested on my view that Bitcoin is fundamentally not a viable currency or a wealth-store hedge against inflation. I also believed that COIN's valuation required unrealistic income growth, and that a true fiat-currency devaluation (that many believe would benefit Bitcoin) would operationally harm Coinbase and drive competition into lower cost platforms. On the back of its disappointing Q2 earnings report, I believe it is an ideal time to reconsider my previous views and take an updated outlook on the company and the cryptocurrency backdrop.

Retail Slowing as Institutions See Legitimacy

Bitcoin and other major cryptos drive COIN's immediate performance. The stock's price is highly correlated to Bitcoin as well as its revenue. See below:

(This data precedes the aftermath of the Q2 report.)

Any outlook on Coinbase requires analysis of both the company and the cryptocurrency market. Higher crypto prices usually coincide with more trading activity, inflows from new accounts, higher transaction commissions, and more investor interest in the crypto landscape. The fact that Coinbase's segment revenues fell in Q2 despite a rebound in Bitcoin is notable. Of course, the non-Bitcoin cryptos did not perform as well until Q3 began.

In the past, I've noted that Bitcoin's performance is usually inversely correlated to individual investor cash allocation levels. This index is measured by the American Association of Individual Investors as representing the higher-wealth segment of retail investors, not necessarily representing those buying Bitcoin; however, it is a decent proxy. Over the past decade, Bitcoin's peaks and troughs usually align with the opposite in cash allocation levels:

My bearish take on Bitcoin last year was based on relatively low cash allocation levels. In my view, Bitcoin and most cryptos act as sinks for excess liquidity. People with spare cash and sufficient equity exposure (or uninterested in equities) park some money into cryptocurrencies. When people have more cash, they have greater crypto buying power.

Of course, despite lower cash allocations in 2024, Bitcoin has had tremendous performance. We were starting to see negative pressure around last fall, as I expected, but that quickly reversed after the November election. I did not appreciate how much Trump's cryptocurrency alignment mattered to Bitcoin investors. In general, I've viewed the Trump-crypto trade as a "buy the rumor, sell the news" trend.

Trump has pursued a handful of orders and legislation that have arguably benefited the crypto market. In January, he issued an order to promote dollar-backed stablecoins while prohibiting agencies from creating CBDCs. He later created the much-speculated Strategic Bitcoin Reserve, promising not to sell confiscated Bitcoin. This month, we've seen the GENIUS Act, creating a law for stablecoins, providing clearer market structure rules for exchanges like Coinbase. Trump has also appointed a number of pro-crypto leaders to agencies, such as the SEC. On Wednesday, Trump issued a 160-page document that outlines regulatory needs, supporting Trump's goal to make the US the "crypto capital of the world."

On one hand, I've argued that these policies do not directly benefit Bitcoin by pushing significant liquidity into the market, as many had hoped. I've also seen increased regulation as delegitimizing Bitcoin's status as a "decentralized alternative currency" free from government regulation and oversight, which was its original purpose. However, by enacting regulatory frameworks, Trump has legitimized Bitcoin for larger institutional investors like banks. With that, we've also seen tremendous growth in the cryptocurrency ETF market.

Liquidity fundamentals remain potentially supportive. AAII cash allocation levels are declining, implying cash is flowing into risk assets (bond allocations are also falling). The US dollar is weakening due to a higher rate cut probability and a potential Fed chair replacement. The US monetary base, or the total amount of currency (physical and Fed-created), is also rising as the Fed quietly winds down its tightening schedule. To a degree, a weaker dollar and higher monetary base are historically bullish for BTC:

Monetary conditions are potentially bullish for Bitcoin. Liquidity was somewhat tight last year, yet Bitcoin still rose. In my view, it benefited dramatically from higher inflows through ETFs and improved institutional support through regulation. We can visualize this by looking at the units outstanding for, by far, the largest Bitcoin ETF (IBIT). IBIT's flows were very strong post-election, slowing during the spring correction, with a sharp uptrend during the recovery. See below:

IBIT's flows today are slowing compared to the May to mid-July trend. That could be a bearish signal, but it is quite early and may reverse. Bitcoin has also been pinned under $120K since mid-July, potentially as recent crypto legislation proves to be a "buy the rumor sell the news" trade.

Although liquidity conditions are comparatively positive and may be fueling a "melt-up" in most risk assets, they are offset by weak economic conditions for median households. As detailed in a few recent articles, since late 2022, consumer trends have been weakening for the median American consumer. Savings levels are chronically low, confidence is weak, and hiring is abysmal. Home prices are also potentially falling. That said, although median data is weak, mean data is decent; average wage growth is good, but the median is not.

The United States is the largest market for Bitcoin, but it is a global asset. To that end, a weaker US dollar should benefit it, though that may depend on whether or not the falling dollar coincides with a recession. In my view, if we assume economic strength is fading in the US, that is likely to be seen in most other high-income countries. Some may argue that an economic slowdown will not harm Bitcoin. That may be true in the long run, particularly if a recession fuels excessive rate cuts and quantitative easing. However, in most cases, Bitcoin and COIN decline with risk assets such as equities.

I do not have an outlook on Bitcoin and think it should remain "sticky" around the $100K level. Some monetary/liquidity factors may push it higher; however, lower inflows into IBIT, Trump's volatile political stock, and seemingly underappreciated economic risks (weaker household savings fundamentals) give me a somewhat bearish long-term outlook.

Coinbase's Regulatory Moat Expands

Compared to last year, I argue that Coinbase faces greater competitive pressure from cryptocurrency ETFs. Spot BTC funds typically have near-zero trading costs and expense ratios well under 50 bps. For long-term buy-and-hold investors, I think IBIT makes more sense than Coinbase's fees, which can be over 2% round-trip. IBIT is huge, with an AUM of over $86B. Coinbase may be around $330B, but the growth rate is stronger for the crypto ETFs.

Now, IBIT and others are not direct competitors because they often pay Coinbase to be a custodian. Its latest investor call noted it powers over 80% of the custody for crypto ETF issuers, as well as a partner to over 150 government agencies looking to manage crypto assets. Coinbase receives a ~0.2% fee for this, something, but far less than it receives from retail transactions.

Coinbase has shifted its focus toward "Crypto-as-a-service", seeing some success in Coinbase One, the USDC stablecoin, the layer 2 network, which aims to make Bitcoin more scalable (potentially solving the transaction and energy issue), and a derivatives market.

The expansion of these products has led to more people seeing Coinbase as the central backbone of the cryptocurrency market. With the GENIUS Act and other orders/legislation providing a more robust regulatory framework, I see that Coinbase is ideally positioned for this. Its market capitalization gives it vast acquisition buying power, allowing it to acquire firms like Derbit easily.

Banks that once scoffed at cryptocurrencies and mocked Coinbase are now becoming close partners. On Wednesday, Coinbase and JP Morgan (JPM) announced a deal to make crypto purchases easier for JP Morgan customers, using Chase credit cards on that platform, or transferring Chase reward points. We saw a similar deal with PNC Bank (PNC) earlier in July.

Crypto ETF proliferation may cut into Coinbase's revenue from retail trading, but altogether, we see a transition from a retail focus toward an institutional one. Ultimately, there is far more liquidity potential from institutions than from retail investors. This gives Coinbase strong growth avenues and a tremendous moat over its competitors.

Despite that, I find the centralized institutional support and regulatory framework that benefits Coinbase problematic. I first heard of Bitcoin around 2015, seeing its popularity surge as a decentralized alternative technology that would end government control of currency. I argue that although Trump's efforts benefit many cryptos and Coinbase, they go directly against the original mission of Bitcoin. Borrowing from the White House press release:

The GENIUS Act prioritizes consumer protection, strengthens the U.S. dollar’s reserve currency status, and bolsters our national security.

Fundamentally, these policies are aimed at strengthening the fiat currency's position within a cryptocurrency market. Years ago, there was a clear divide between government/institutions viewing cryptocurrency as a potential threat and people buying Bitcoin as a rebellious hedge against fiat money volatility.

In my opinion, there is a legitimate risk that the fundamental bull case behind cryptocurrencies is broken through excessive regulatory oversight. Retail investors may sell as a result, and it is unclear if institutional support is strong enough to provide "exit liquidity". In the short term, new regulations are supportive for Bitcoin and secure Coinbase's competitive moat, but I continue to doubt its long-term viability.

The Bottom Line

My outlook for Bitcoin and other major cryptocurrencies is neutral. Specifically, I see a bearish bias among data relevant to retail crypto flows, relevant to Coinbase in the short-term, offset by supportive trends for institutional flows. The latter includes improved regulation, a shift back toward pro-inflation/liquidity monetary policies, and altogether improved legitimacy for banks and investment funds.

It is unclear if that tailwind offsets the seemingly weaker investment savings capacity for median-income people and potentially low sideline cash that could go into cryptocurrencies. Retail investor cash allocations are lower, but are still falling - implying retail inflows are still available, but not for long. The recent stagnation in major crypto ETF inflows also supports that.

My view on Coinbase is similar. I expect the company may see more pressure on its retail trading fee segment with lower account growth, but its deals with institutions may provide superior verticals/niches. Still, its valuation is extremely high. The company is worth about as much as CME Group (CME) and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE). Its forward "P/E" is much higher at 67X, with CME at about 25X and ICE ~27X; however, its forward price-to-sales is between the two:

Although Coinbase's valuation is extremely high, it is generally reasonable compared to that of CME and ICE. I argue that COIN is overvalued because its risks are greater, given that CME and ICE operate markets that have existed since the 17th century, while COIN's is around fifteen years old. The counterargument is that Coinbase's revenue has more growth potential because of its youth, and Coinbase has a stronger competitive moat.

I remain skeptical that the cryptocurrency market is little more than a glorified gambling industry benefiting from excessive liquidity generation through quantitative easing. I think it is far from its roots as a decentralized currency alternative. In my experience, most people who buy it seem to do so to speculate on a higher price, not out of a genuine desire to hedge against fiat currency risks.

I would not buy COIN today, and suspect it will eventually decline further. However, in the short run, we have a small discount after its Q2 disappointment. My bearish view is long-term, expecting a fall within years, but not necessarily months. Its short-term performance is driven by BTC, which I feel is futile to predict. At this point, Q3 is likely to show improvement, given that Bitcoin is up while Ethereum is nearly 50% higher. I suspect its Q2 weakness may be due to lower retail buying power and a shift in flows toward ETFs. This is likely its greatest risk in Q3, offset by its significant expansion efforts and widening range of services and institutional partnerships.

data.title: Dollar Weakness Boosts International Appeal
data.description:
By Kristy Akullian, CFA

A Case For Going International

International stocks trounced U.S. equity markets in the first half of 2025, in one of the largest performance gaps in decades (Figure 1). While we still believe many U.S. companies are exceptional, and we continue to believe in the growth potential of the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, we see three reasons for U.S. investors to consider increased exposures to international stocks, as detailed below: the dollar’s downturn, concentration risk in U.S. large-cap indexes like the S&P 500, and the value tilt of international equities.

Figure 1: U.S. vs. International performance

The Declining Dollar

The dollar is down nearly 10% year to date.1 Still, we believe it has farther to fall. The dollar has long benefited from a cycle of robust U.S. growth and investment, attracting foreign capital which in turn strengthened the dollar, creating a virtuous cycle. However, we think the dollar’s structural bull market that has persisted since 2014 may be coming to an end as American exceptionalism is challenged. Unpredictable trade policy, a reorientation of global growth, or demand for alternative reserve currencies could all weaken foreign demand for U.S. assets, putting further downward pressure on the dollar.

Over the last decade, a strong dollar has dampened international returns for a U.S.-based investor. Consider Figure 2, where unhedged international developed market returns (MSCI EAFE Index) benefited from currency impacts in just two of the preceding 10 years. Should our expectations for further dollar weakness be realized, U.S. investors could face a sea change in their experience of international investing, where currency becomes a tailwind to bolster returns rather than a headwind to diminish them.

Figure 2: Dollar strength has historically been a drag on international equity returns

U.S. Concentration Risk

The so-called Magnificent Seven stocks have delivered remarkable returns over the past three years, fueled by the promise of AI and the unrivaled ability of U.S. corporations to generate outsized profits.2 Strong performance from the top has also improved the quality attributes of broad indexes, reducing average leverage and increasing profitability metrics.3 In a world of structurally higher interest rates, this continues to underpin our preference for high quality, primarily large cap, U.S. companies.

The flip side to this remarkable performance, however, is a historic rise in concentration: market cap-weighted indices like the S&P 500 are as concentrated as they have ever been. Thirty years ago, the top 20 names in the S&P 500 represented about 28% of the total index. Today, the top 20 names account for over 40%.4

The same phenomenon is happening at a global level as well, where the share of U.S. equities in global indexes has also reached record highs. In 2005, the U.S. made up just 50% of the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) universe (Figure 3). Twenty years of American exceptionalism later, that number has climbed to 64% (Figure 3).

While an overweight position in U.S. large caps has paid off over the last decade and a half, concentration risk is still a top cited concern amongst investors we speak to. As leadership narrows, headline-level returns are increasingly beholden to a smaller list of constituents and countries, potentially making portfolios riskier overall.

Alongside the tailwind of a declining dollar, we favor international indexes as a diversification tool precisely because they have bucked the concentration trend. Since 2005, the three largest countries in MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index — the U.K., Japan and France — have steadily ceded ground, with their combined weight declining as leadership has broadened out.5

Figure 3.1: Concentration swells in the U.S.

Figure 3.2: While declining elsewhere

As investors look to build more balanced portfolios — with H1’s performance a stark reminder of why — we prefer international equities as potential diversifiers to large-cap U.S. stocks. International equities have delivered both lower volatility and lower correlation relative to the S&P 500 than small caps over the last decade.6 That relationship is emphasized in drawdown periods: since 2010, when the S&P 500 posted negative quarterly returns, small caps underperformed significantly — posting nearly double the losses of developed markets. In fact, since 1999, in months when the S&P 500 was down, small caps were always down by more than international equities.7

In addition to their dimmer diversification prospects, the macro outlook also remains a headwind for small caps. Smaller, riskier companies have tended to perform best when we are coming out of a recession — not when we see U.S. growth slowing in the quarters ahead.8

Factors Favor International Stocks

International markets often have different drivers of risk and return than U.S. ones. One way to quantify these differences is by factor breakdown. The U.S. has long benefitted from its inherent growth tilt, while both developed and emerging markets often exhibit higher exposure to the value factor, with higher dividend and earnings yields than their U.S. counterparts.9 By incorporating international equities into a diversified portfolio, investors may benefit from structural geopolitical or thematic forces, while also offsetting some of the inherent growth bias within their U.S. equity sleeves.

The alpha generated by factor tilting is even greater in international markets than it is in U.S. ones, making factor investing a compelling implementation style for those looking to add exposure. Currently, we favor the quality factor in international markets, which screens for companies that have the earnings stability and cash flows to weather a volatile geopolitical backdrop. The quality factor also exhibits an asymmetric up/down capture profile — meaning it has historically participated to a greater degree on the upside than on the downside.10

Conclusion

Home country bias is a perennial theme among U.S. investor portfolios. The average advisor is overweight U.S. equities, with allocations continually increasing over the past decade. Our analysis of more than 20,000 portfolios showed that, on average, advisors allocate as much as 77% of their stock sleeves to U.S. companies11 — compared to a 64% weighting in MSCI All Country World Index.12

But recent polling data suggests that trend may soon reverse.13 Many advisors see opportunity in international equities: the exposure captured the largest share of bullish sentiment in our most recent poll with 43% considering adds in the next 3-6 months. We believe that increased demand could be a catalyst for a further move higher.

U.S. investors interested in adding to international exposures via the ease and tax-efficiency of exchange traded funds (ETFs) may consider:

  • The iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (IXUS) provides exposure to a broad range of international developed and emerging market companies. IXUS seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of large-, mid- and small-capitalization non-U.S. equities.
  • The iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV) provides low cost exposure to over 20 global developed markets, excluding the U.S. IDEV seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of large-, mid- and small-capitalization developed market equities, excluding the United States.
  • Investors specifically interested in accessing the quality factor in international markets may consider the iShares MSCI Intl Quality Factor ETF (IQLT), which provides exposure to developed international stocks exhibiting positive fundamentals: high return on equity, stable year-over-year earnings growth and low financial leverage.

© 2025 BlackRock, Inc. All rights reserved.

1 Bloomberg, as of June 23, 2025, U.S. Dollar Spot Index.

2 Magnificent 7 stocks representative of Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla. As of June 23, 2025. Specific companies or issuers are mentioned for educational purposes only and should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities. Any companies mentioned do not necessarily represent current or future holdings of any BlackRock products. For actual fund holdings, please visit the respective fund product pages.

3 Source: Morningstar, as of June 23, 2025.

4 Bloomberg. As of June 23, 2025.

5 Bloomberg. As of June 23, 2025.

6 Bloomberg, as of June 23, 2025.

7 Bloomberg, as of June 23, 2025. Small caps represented by the Russell 2000 Index. Index performance is for illustrative purposes only. Index performance does not reflect any management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

8 Bloomberg, as of June 23, 2025. Small caps represented by the Russell 2000 Index. Index performance is for illustrative purposes only. Index performance does not reflect any management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

9 BlackRock, Morningstar, as of 12/31/2024.

10 Bloomberg, as of June 23, 2025. Quality represented by the MSCI World ex USA Sector Neutral Quality Index. Up/down capture ratios explain how well investments do when the market goes up or down. Up capture is how much the investment gains when the broad market rises, like the Foreign Large Blend Morningstar Category in this case, and down capture is how much the investment losses when the market was falling.

11 Source: Morningstar, BlackRock, Aladdin. “Advisor models” data is as of 5/31/25. “BLK model” allocations as of 5/20/25. For illustrative purposes only. Allocation data compares the Target Allocation Hybrid with Alternatives 60/40 fixed income and alternative model holdings as of 2/27/25 to the average fixed income and alternatives sleeve across 4,984 “moderate” risk cohort models collected by BlackRock in the 12 months ending 4/30/25. Duration compares the average duration of the 4,984 “moderate” advisor models collected by BlackRock in the 12 months ending 4/30/25 with the Target Allocation ETF 60/40 model portfolio duration as of 5/20/25. Advisor models collected by BlackRock are grouped into 5 risk cohorts for analysis, based on total equity allocation. Models in the “moderate” risk cohort are defined as any portfolio with an overall equity allocation of between 50-65%. BlackRock’s risk model data is supplemented by asset allocation and fund characteristic data from Morningstar. The portfolios analyzed represent a subset of the industry, and not its entirety. As such, there may be certain biases present in the data that reflect the advisors who choose to work with BlackRock to analyze their portfolios. See Important Notes slide for descriptions of each category. The Target Allocation Hybrid with Alternatives portfolio shown should not be relied upon as research, investment advice or a recommendation regarding Funds or any security in particular. This information is strictly for illustrative and educational purposes and is subject to change. Allocations are targets and subject to change.

12 Source: MSCI as of June 1, 2025.

13 Insights from BlackRock client polling data, as of June 23, 2025. Findings are based on responses collected from a sample of 560 unique participants.

Carefully consider the Funds' investment objectives, risk factors, and charges and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the Funds' prospectuses or, if available, the summary prospectuses, which may be obtained by visiting the iShares Fund and BlackRock Fund prospectus pages. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Investing involves risk, including possible loss of principal.

This material is not intended to be relied upon as a forecast, research or investment advice, and is not a recommendation, offer or solicitation to buy or sell any securities or to adopt any investment strategy. The opinions expressed are as of the date indicated and may change as subsequent conditions vary. The information and opinions contained in this material are derived from proprietary and non-proprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy. As such, no warranty of accuracy or reliability is given and no responsibility arising in any other way for errors and omissions (including responsibility to any person by reason of negligence) is accepted by BlackRock, its officers, employees or agents. This material may contain “forward-looking” information that is not purely historical in nature. Such information may include, among other things, projections and forecasts. There is no guarantee that any of these views will come to pass. Reliance upon information in this material is at the sole discretion of the viewer.

Diversification and asset allocation may not protect against market risk or loss of principal.

Active funds are subject to management risk, which means the fund manager's techniques may not produce desired results, and the selected securities may not align with the fund's investment objective. Legislative, regulatory, or tax developments may also affect the fund manager's ability to achieve the investment objective.

There can be no assurance that performance will be enhanced or risk will be reduced for funds that seek to provide exposure to certain quantitative investment characteristics ("factors"). Exposure to such investment factors may detract from performance in some market environments, perhaps for extended periods. In such circumstances, a fund may seek to maintain exposure to the targeted investment factors and not adjust to target different factors, which could result in losses.

International investing involves risks, including risks related to foreign currency, limited liquidity, less government regulation and the possibility of substantial volatility due to adverse political, economic or other developments. These risks often are heightened for investments in emerging/ developing markets or in concentrations of single countries.

Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in the value of debt securities. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the debt issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments.

The Funds are distributed by BlackRock Investments, LLC (together with its affiliates, "BlackRock").

None of these companies make any representation regarding the advisability of investing in the Funds.
